section.title: Overview
section.para: The cachedb_nats module implements the &osips; CacheDB engine interface on top of NATS JetStream Key-Value stores. It provides persistent, replicated, cluster-aware key-value storage accessible from &osips; routing scripts via the standard cache_store/cache_fetch/cache_remove operations, together with several NATS-native extensions.

The module connects to a NATS JetStream-enabled server or cluster, creates a named KV bucket on first use, and maps all CacheDB operations onto NATS KV primitives. Reconnection and bucket handle refresh are fully automatic: an epoch-based staleness check at the top of every operation transparently re-opens the KV handle after any NATS disconnect/reconnect cycle.
Beyond the standard CacheDB API the module provides:

listitem.para: Atomic CAS counters — cache_add and cache_sub use compare-and-swap with up to three retries, giving safe concurrent counter increments from multiple &osips; worker processes.

JSON full-text search index — keys whose names begin with fts_json_prefix are automatically parsed and indexed in a process-local hash table. cache_query performs O(1) equality lookups against this index without scanning the KV bucket.
Live KV watcher — a self-healing pthread watches the KV bucket in real time, updating the search index on every put/delete/purge and raising the E_NATS_KV_CHANGE EVI event for script reaction.
nats_request() — synchronous NATS request/reply (RPC) callable from routing scripts.
nats_kv_history() — retrieves the full version history of a KV key as a JSON array.
Raw query commands — KV KEYS, KV PURGE, and KV BUCKET INFO via cache_raw_query.
Map operations — composite key:subkey addressing for get, set, and remove of individual fields within JSON documents.

section.title: &osips; Modules
section.para: The following modules must be loaded before this module:

listitem.para: None are required. The module links dynamically against libnats_pool.so in lib/nats (installed alongside the module .so files and located at load time via an $ORIGIN RUNPATH), and registers directly with the &osips; CacheDB and EVI subsystems in the core. No other &osips; module needs to be loaded first.

section.para: The following modules are only needed for specific script-level uses:

listitem.para: event_nats — required only if a script forwards E_NATS_KV_CHANGE (or any other event) to a NATS subject via subscribe_event with a nats: transport. For purely local consumption through an event_route block the core EVI subsystem handles dispatch and event_nats is not needed.

section.para: When more than one NATS module (cachedb_nats, event_nats, nats_consumer, ...) is loaded in the same &osips; instance, they all share a single libnats_pool.so instance and therefore a single set of pool globals (pool_cfg, _nc, _js, KV handle cache). The first nats_pool_register call at mod_init time seeds the connection parameters (seed URL list, TLS options, reconnection tuning); subsequent calls from other modules only append any additional seed URLs and otherwise do not override the configuration. Load the module whose nats_url / TLS parameters you want to take effect first in opensips.cfg.

section.title: External Libraries or Applications
section.para: The following libraries must be installed before running &osips; with this module loaded:

listitem.para: nats.c v3.13 or later (libnats.so):

The official C client library for NATS, available at https://github.com/nats-io/nats.c. Build with CMake and install to a standard library path (e.g. /usr/local/lib/libnats.so). Version 3.13 or later is required for JetStream KV support.

For TLS connections, nats.c must be built against OpenSSL. OpenSSL is the only TLS backend supported by upstream nats.c: WolfSSL, mbedTLS, and other TLS libraries are not supported by the library's build system. The same OpenSSL installation used by &osips; must be present at runtime to avoid library version conflicts.

NATS Server 2.9 or later with JetStream enabled (jetstream: {} in the server configuration). For production deployments a three-node JetStream cluster is recommended to survive single-node failures with kv_replicas set to 3.

section.title: cachedb_url (string)
section.para: The optional group label (e.g. nats:mycluster://) distinguishes multiple cache namespaces in the same script. Multiple comma-separated host[:port] pairs may be supplied as a bootstrap seed list; nats.c discovers the remainder of the cluster via INFO gossip. An optional user[:password] section supplies NATS user/password authentication credentials which are passed through verbatim to the nats.c client (for token-based authentication, place the token in the user field with no password).
When nats_url is also set it takes precedence over the host/credentials portion of this URL for the actual NATS connection; the URL is then used solely for group registration.
This parameter may be set more than once to register multiple cache group names backed by the same NATS connection.

example.title: Set cachedb_url parameter
example.programlisting:
... # Plain, single node modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats://127.0.0.1:4222/")
# Named group modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats:calls://127.0.0.1:4222/")
# User/password authentication modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats://alice:s3cret@nats.example.com:4222/")
# Multi-host seed list modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats://nats-1:4222,nats-2:4222,nats-3:4222/") ...

example.title: Use the nats cache group in a routing script

section.title: nats_url (string)
section.para: Comma-separated list of NATS server URLs used as the bootstrap seed list for the connection pool. Each entry follows the native nats.c URL form nats://[user[:password]@]host[:port] (or tls://... to request TLS explicitly). Token authentication is expressed as a user without a password. After the initial connection is established, nats.c discovers all cluster peers via the INFO protocol gossip mechanism and maintains the full topology automatically, so this parameter only needs to contain enough servers to bootstrap.
Use DNS hostnames rather than IP addresses so that getaddrinfo re-resolves on each reconnect attempt, automatically following topology changes in the cluster.
When this parameter is set it takes precedence over the host and credentials portion of cachedb_url.

example.title: Set nats_url parameter
example.programlisting:
... # Three-node cluster seed list modparam("cachedb_nats", "nats_url", "nats://nats-1:4222,nats://nats-2:4222,nats://nats-3:4222")
# User/password authentication modparam("cachedb_nats", "nats_url", "nats://alice:s3cret@nats.example.com:4222")
# Token authentication (user field only, no password) modparam("cachedb_nats", "nats_url", "nats://s3cret-token@nats.example.com:4222") ...

section.title: kv_bucket (string)
section.para: The name of the NATS JetStream KV bucket that this module will use for all key-value operations. If the bucket does not yet exist it will be created during child_init with the replication, history, and TTL settings defined by the kv_replicas, kv_history, and kv_ttl parameters.

If the bucket already exists the creation parameters are ignored and the existing bucket configuration is used as-is. To change replication on an existing bucket use the NATS CLI: nats kv update <bucket> --replicas=N.
Bucket names must be valid NATS subject tokens (alphanumeric, hyphens, and underscores; no spaces or dots).

section.title: kv_replicas (integer)
section.para: The JetStream replication factor for the KV bucket. A value of 3 means every key-value entry is replicated to three NATS nodes, tolerating single-node failure without data loss. This parameter only takes effect when the bucket is first created; it is ignored if the bucket already exists.
For development or single-node deployments use a value of 1.

section.title: kv_history (integer)
section.para: The number of value revisions to retain per key. A higher value allows nats_kv_history to return more historical entries and enables the CAS retry loop in atomic counter operations to succeed under higher write contention. This parameter only takes effect when the bucket is first created.

section.title: kv_ttl (integer)
section.para: Bucket-level TTL in seconds. When greater than zero, all keys in the bucket automatically expire after this many seconds since their last modification. A value of zero disables expiry.

Note: NATS KV TTL is bucket-wide. Per-key TTL is not supported by the NATS JetStream KV API. The expires argument to cache_store and cache_add is accepted for API compatibility but does not set a per-key TTL. This parameter only takes effect when the bucket is first created.

section.title: fts_json_prefix (string)
section.para: Key name prefix that marks documents for inclusion in the in-process JSON full-text search index. Any key stored via cache_store whose name begins with this prefix is parsed as a JSON object and indexed for equality queries via cache_query.

The index maps every top-level string and integer field of the JSON document to the document key, enabling O(1) hash lookups. Documents stored without this prefix are still accessible via the standard KV operations but are not indexed.

section.title: fts_max_results (integer)
section.para: The maximum number of documents returned by a single cache_query call. Limits memory consumption for broad queries that would otherwise match a large fraction of the index.

section.title: kv_watch (string)
section.para: A NATS subject pattern that restricts the KV watcher to a subset of keys. NATS wildcard tokens are supported: > matches any remaining subject tokens (e.g. usrloc.> watches all keys under usrloc.), and * matches exactly one token. When this parameter is not set, the watcher subscribes to all keys in the bucket.

This parameter may be set more than once to watch multiple disjoint key patterns simultaneously. Each call adds one pattern to the watch list; all patterns are passed to kvStore_WatchMulti as a combined subscription. If no kv_watch parameters are set, the watcher is not started.

para.emphasis: Default value is NULL (watcher disabled unless at least one kv_watch pattern is configured).

section.title: tls_ca (string)
section.para: Path to a PEM-encoded CA certificate file used to verify the NATS server's TLS certificate. When this parameter is set, the module automatically upgrades the NATS connection URL from nats:// to tls://.

section.title: tls_cert (string)
section.para: Path to the PEM-encoded client certificate for mutual TLS authentication with the NATS server. Required only when the NATS server is configured to demand client certificates (verify: true).

section.title: tls_key (string)
section.para: Path to the PEM-encoded private key corresponding to tls_cert.

section.title: tls_hostname (string)
section.para: Override the expected TLS server hostname for certificate verification. Required when connecting to a NATS server by IP address, since nats.c has NATS_FORCE_HOST_VERIFICATION enabled by default and the server certificate typically has DNS Subject Alternative Names only (no IP SANs).

para.emphasis: Default value is NULL (use the hostname from the URL).

section.title: tls_skip_verify (integer)
section.para: When set to 1, disables TLS server certificate verification. Useful for development environments with self-signed certificates. Do not use in production.

section.title: reconnect_wait (integer)
section.para: The time in milliseconds to wait between reconnection attempts when the NATS connection is lost. Applies to the shared connection pool managed by lib/nats.

section.title: max_reconnect (integer)
section.para: Maximum number of reconnection attempts during child_init. Once the initial connection is established, the nats.c library handles runtime reconnection autonomously with no limit. This parameter affects only the startup retry loop, not runtime resilience.

section.title: skip_openssl_init (integer)
section.para: When set to 1, instructs nats.c not to call SSL_library_init and related OpenSSL global initialisation functions. This prevents double-initialisation conflicts between nats.c and the OpenSIPS TLS subsystem, which also manages OpenSSL. This flag should remain enabled (the default) in all deployments where &osips; is compiled with TLS support.

Performs a synchronous NATS request/reply operation. Publishes payload to the NATS subject subject and waits up to timeout_ms milliseconds for a reply. When a reply arrives the payload is stored in result_pvar.

This function implements the standard NATS request/reply pattern: the sender publishes to a subject and includes a unique reply-to inbox. A responding service publishes to that inbox. This is suitable for synchronous RPC calls to external services such as authentication validators, fraud scoring engines, or routing decision services.
This function can be used from any route.
Parameters:

listitem.para: payload (string) — Request payload, typically a JSON string. May contain pseudo-variables.
timeout_ms (integer) — Maximum wait time in milliseconds. If no reply is received within this window the function returns -2.
result_pvar (writable pvar) — Pseudo-variable to receive the reply payload string.

section.para: Return values:

listitem.para: 1 — reply received successfully.
-1 — error (NATS connection failure or publish error).
-2 — timeout, no reply received within timeout_ms.

example.title: nats_request usage

section.para: Retrieves the revision history for a KV key and stores it as a JSON array string in result_pvar. Each array element is an object with the fields rev (revision number, integer), value (the stored string at that revision), and ts (Unix timestamp of the revision in nanoseconds).
The depth of history available depends on the kv_history bucket setting. With the default of 5, up to five past values are returned per key in reverse chronological order (most recent first).
This function can be used from any route.
Parameters:

listitem.para: key (string) — KV key to retrieve history for. May contain pseudo-variables.
result_pvar (writable pvar) — Pseudo-variable to receive the JSON history array.

section.para: Return values:

listitem.para: 1 — history retrieved successfully.
-1 — error (NATS failure or allocation error).
-2 — key not found or no history available.

section.title: CacheDB Operations
section.para: All standard &osips; CacheDB script functions are supported. The engine identifier in every call must match the group name used in cachedb_url (default: nats).

section.title: cache_store — KV put
section.para: Stores a string value under the given key in the NATS KV bucket. The key must consist of valid NATS subject tokens (alphanumeric, hyphens, underscores, and dots as separators; no spaces or colons). If the key already exists it is overwritten.

When the key begins with fts_json_prefix and the value is a valid JSON object, the document is parsed and added to the in-process search index so that it becomes immediately queryable via cache_query.

example.title: cache_store with nats
example.programlisting:
... # Plain key-value cache_store("nats", "call.$ci", "$fu|$tu");
# JSON document (auto-indexed because key starts with "json:") cache_store("nats", "json.user.alice", "{\"name\":\"alice\",\"domain\":\"example.com\",\"status\":\"active\"}"); ...

section.title: cache_fetch — KV get
section.para: Retrieves the current value of a key from the NATS KV bucket and stores it in the given pseudo-variable. Returns 1 on success, -2 if the key does not exist, and -1 on error.

example.title: cache_fetch with nats

section.title: cache_remove — KV delete
section.para: Removes a key from the NATS KV bucket. The operation is a soft delete that creates a deletion tombstone while preserving revision history. Use the KV PURGE raw query command to permanently remove a key and its full history.

section.title: cache_add — atomic counter increment
section.para: Atomically increments an integer counter stored in the KV bucket. The operation uses compare-and-swap (CAS) with up to three retry attempts, safely handling concurrent increments from multiple &osips; worker processes. If the key does not yet exist it is created with an initial value equal to increment.

The counter is stored as a plain decimal integer string in the KV bucket. The expires argument is accepted for API compatibility but does not set a per-key TTL (NATS KV TTL is bucket-wide; see kv_ttl).

example.title: cache_add with nats

section.title: cache_sub — atomic counter decrement
section.para: Atomically decrements an integer counter. Equivalent to cache_add with a negated increment value. Uses CAS with three retries.

section.title: cache_counter_fetch — read counter value
section.para: Reads the current integer value of a counter key. Returns 1 on success (counter value in pvar), -2 if the key does not exist, and -1 on error.

example.title: cache_counter_fetch with nats

section.title: JSON Full-Text Search Index
section.para: The cachedb_nats module maintains a process-local hash-based search index over JSON documents stored in the KV bucket. Documents are included in the index when their key begins with the string defined by fts_json_prefix (default: json:). All top-level string and integer fields of each document are indexed as field:value entries mapping to the document key.
The index supports equality queries via the standard CacheDB cache_query call:

example.title: cache_query — JSON equality search

listitem.para: Startup: the index is built by scanning the entire KV bucket during child_init. Every key matching fts_json_prefix is fetched and parsed.
Live updates: the KV watcher thread calls nats_json_index_add or nats_json_index_remove for every put, delete, or purge event received from the NATS server.

Reconnection: when the watcher detects that a reconnect epoch change has occurred, it performs a full nats_json_index_rebuild — clearing all existing entries and repopulating from the current KV state. This guarantees the index never drifts after a disconnect.

section.para: Thread safety: all index operations acquire a dedicated pthread_mutex_t. The KV watcher thread (writer) and &osips; SIP worker processes (readers via cache_query) contend on this mutex. Index memory is managed with raw malloc and free rather than &osips; pkg_malloc, because the watcher pthread and SIP worker thread share the same process address space and pkg_malloc is not thread-safe across pthreads. Statistics counters use __atomic_fetch_add for lock-free cross-thread accumulation.
The maximum number of results returned per query is controlled by fts_max_results.

section.title: KV Watcher
section.para: The KV watcher is a self-healing pthread that runs inside the first &osips; SIP worker process (rank 1) and monitors the NATS KV bucket for real-time mutations. It is started by nats_watch_start during child_init when at least one kv_watch pattern is configured.

Only a single watcher per &osips; instance is started (on rank 1) to minimise JetStream ordered consumer count. Each watcher creates one JetStream ordered push consumer in the NATS server; during cluster topology changes nats.c rebalances these consumers internally, and fewer consumers reduce the window for race conditions during failover.
The watcher follows a four-phase loop:

listitem.para: Phase 1 — Wait for connectivity: polls nats_pool_is_connected until the NATS connection is established. Sleeps briefly between polls to avoid busy-waiting.

Phase 2 — Rebuild index: acquires a fresh KV handle from the pool and calls nats_json_index_rebuild to repopulate the search index from the current bucket contents. This is the crash-recovery path that ensures the index is consistent after any disconnect.
Phase 3 — Create watcher: calls kvStore_WatchMulti with the configured key patterns to create a kvWatcher handle.

Phase 4 — Process updates: calls kvWatcher_Next in a loop. For each received kvEntry the watcher:
Updates the JSON search index (nats_json_index_add for put, nats_json_index_remove for delete or purge).
Raises an E_NATS_KV_CHANGE EVI event carrying the key, operation type, value (on puts), and NATS revision number.
If a reconnect epoch change is detected the loop exits back to Phase 1.

section.para: The _watcher_running flag is an atomic_int accessed via atomic_load and atomic_store. The nats_watch_stop function signals the thread to exit, stops the kvWatcher to unblock kvWatcher_Next, then joins the thread. It is safe to call during &osips; shutdown regardless of whether the watcher was ever started.

section.title: Raw Query Commands
section.para: The module exposes NATS KV administrative operations via the standard CacheDB cache_raw_query interface. The following commands are supported:

section.title: KV KEYS
section.para: Lists all keys currently present in the KV bucket. The result is stored in the provided pseudo-variable as a comma-separated string of key names.

section.title: KV PURGE <key>
section.para: Permanently removes a key and its entire revision history from the KV bucket. Unlike a soft delete (which leaves a deletion tombstone), a purge is irreversible and frees the storage used by all historical revisions.

section.title: KV BUCKET INFO
section.para: Returns a JSON object describing the KV bucket configuration: bucket name, replication factor, history depth, TTL, and the backing JetStream stream name.

section.title: Map Operations
section.para: The module supports composite key:subkey addressing for reading and writing individual fields of JSON documents stored in the KV bucket. These operations are accessible via the column-oriented CacheDB API functions (map_get, map_set, map_remove) which are used internally by &osips; modules such as usrloc when configured with a CacheDB backend.
map_get — retrieves the JSON document at key and returns its top-level fields as separate columns in a cdb_res_t result set.
map_set — reads the existing JSON document at key (or starts from an empty object), merges the provided field-value pairs, and writes the result back using CAS for consistency.
map_remove — reads the existing JSON document, removes the named field (subkey), and writes the result back. If no fields remain after removal the entire key is deleted from the bucket.
Returns a JSON object with the current KV bucket configuration and NATS connection state. Useful for monitoring and diagnostics.
Response fields:

listitem.para: bucket (string) — name of the configured KV bucket.
replicas (integer) — configured replication factor.
history (integer) — configured history depth per key.
ttl (integer) — configured bucket TTL in seconds (0 = no expiry).
connected (string) — yes if the NATS connection pool is currently connected, no otherwise.

section.para: Raised by the KV watcher thread for every mutation received from the NATS KV bucket. This event allows &osips; routing scripts to react to changes made by any client in the cluster — not just changes made through &osips; itself.
The event is raised with the following parameters:

listitem.para: key (string) — the KV key that was modified.
operation (string) — the type of mutation: put (value created or updated), delete (soft delete tombstone), or purge (hard delete with history removal).
value (string) — the new value of the key. Only present for put operations; absent for delete and purge.
revision (integer) — the NATS JetStream sequence number (revision) of this mutation. Revisions are monotonically increasing and globally ordered within the KV bucket.

example.title: Subscribing to E_NATS_KV_CHANGE in opensips.cfg

section.title: Cluster Configuration
section.para: For production deployments a three-node NATS JetStream cluster is recommended. The nats_url parameter is a seed list used only for the initial bootstrap connection. After the first connection is established, nats.c discovers all cluster peers via the INFO protocol gossip mechanism (connect_urls) and maintains the full topology automatically. Adding or removing nodes from the cluster is transparent to the &osips; module.

Use DNS hostnames rather than IP addresses in nats_url. The nats.c library calls getaddrinfo on each reconnection attempt, so DNS-based hostnames automatically pick up topology changes such as node replacement or IP address reassignment.

The kv_replicas parameter controls the JetStream replication factor and only takes effect when the KV bucket is first created. A value of 3 on a three-node cluster tolerates one-node failure without data loss or availability impact. To change the replication factor on an existing bucket use the NATS CLI:

The internal reconnection loop is unlimited at runtime: nats.c will never permanently remove a server from its routing pool. The max_reconnect parameter only limits the number of retries during the initial connection phase in child_init.

example.title: Three-node cluster configuration
example.programlisting:
... loadmodule "cachedb_nats.so"
modparam("cachedb_nats", "cachedb_url", "nats://nats-1:4222/") modparam("cachedb_nats", "nats_url", "nats://nats-1:4222,nats://nats-2:4222,nats://nats-3:4222") modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_bucket", "opensips") modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_replicas", 3) modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_history", 5) modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_ttl", 0) modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_watch", "usrloc.>") modparam("cachedb_nats", "kv_watch", "json.>") ...
